content:
# Exodus Chapter 5 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios ~33v–34r (PDF pp. 65–66); Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 45–46 (LXX) | **Source Image:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/EXO_005_page_001.jpg`, `EXO_005_page_002.jpg`

---

**1** After that, Moses and Aaron went to Pharaoh and said, "This is what YHWH, the God of Israel, says: Let my people go, so they can hold a festival for me in the wilderness."

**2** Pharaoh said, "Who is YHWH, that I should obey him and let Israel go? I do not know YHWH, and I will not let Israel go."

**3** They said, "The God of the Hebrews has met with us. Let us make a three-day journey into the wilderness to offer sacrifices to YHWH our God — or he may strike us with plague or sword."

**4** The king of Egypt said to them, "Moses and Aaron — why are you pulling the people away from their labor? Get back to your forced work!"

**5** And Pharaoh said, "Look at how many these people already are — and you want to give them a rest from their labor?"

**6** That same day, Pharaoh gave orders to the slave drivers and their foremen:

**7** "Stop giving the people straw for making bricks, the way you have been. Let them go and gather their own straw.

**8** But demand the same number of bricks as before — not one fewer. They are lazy. That is why they keep crying out, 'Let us go sacrifice to our God.'

**9** Pile the work on heavier. Keep them grinding. Then they will stop listening to these lies."

**10** So the slave drivers and the foremen went out and told the people: "This is what Pharaoh says: I will no longer supply you with straw.

**11** Go find your own straw wherever you can. But your workload will not be reduced by a single brick."

**12** So the people scattered across the whole land of Egypt, gathering stubble to use as straw.

**13** The slave drivers kept pressing them: "Finish your daily quota — the same as when straw was provided!"

**14** The Israelite foremen, whom Pharaoh's slave drivers had put in charge, were beaten and asked, "Why didn't you meet your brick quota yesterday or today, as you used to?"

**15** The Israelite foremen went and cried out to Pharaoh: "Why are you treating your servants this way?

**16** No straw is given to your servants, yet they tell us, 'Make bricks!' Your own servants are being beaten, but the fault lies with your people."

**17** Pharaoh said, "Lazy — that's what you are. Lazy! That is why you keep saying, 'Let us go sacrifice to YHWH.'

**18** Now get back to work! No straw will be given to you, but you will produce the full count of bricks."

**19** The Israelite foremen could see they were trapped when they were told, "You must not reduce your daily brick output by even one."

**20** As they left Pharaoh, they ran into Moses and Aaron, who were waiting for them.

**21** They said to Moses and Aaron, "May YHWH look on you and judge you! You have made us stink in the nostrils of Pharaoh and his officials. You have put a sword in their hand to kill us."

**22** Moses went back to YHWH and said, "Lord, why have you brought disaster on this people? Why did you ever send me?

**23** From the moment I went to Pharaoh to speak in your name, he has done nothing but harm to this people — and you? You have done nothing at all to rescue them."

---
## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

**v.1 — "Let my people go" (Heb. *shallach et-ammi*, שַׁלַּ֣ח אֶת־עַמִּ֔י):** The most iconic line of the exodus. The Hebrew *shallach* is a strong imperative — "release, send away, let loose." It is not a request but a divine command delivered through human mouths. The full phrase includes purpose: *veyachoggu li* (וְיָחֹ֥גּוּ לִ֖י), "so they may hold a festival for me." The verb *chagag* specifically denotes a pilgrim festival — joyful, communal, involving dance and sacrifice. Pharaoh is not being asked to free slaves permanently (yet) but to release them for worship. The Vaticanus reads *exaposteilon ton laon mou* (ἐξαπόστειλον τὸν λαόν μου) — "send away my people" — with the same force.

**v.2 — "Who is YHWH?" (Heb. *mi YHWH*, מִ֤י יְהוָ֗ה):** Pharaoh's response is not mere ignorance — it is contempt. In the ancient Near East, a king acknowledged only the gods whose authority he recognized. "I do not know YHWH" (*lo yadati et-YHWH*, לֹ֤א יָדַ֨עְתִּי֙ אֶת־יְהוָ֔ה) is a diplomatic refusal of jurisdiction. Pharaoh is saying: this god has no standing in my court. The entire plague narrative will be YHWH's answer to this question — a systematic education in divine identity. The Vaticanus renders it *tis estin hou eisakousomai tes phones autou* (τίς ἐστιν οὗ εἰσακούσομαι τῆς φωνῆς αὐτοῦ) — "who is he that I should listen to his voice?" — adding the dimension of voice/authority.

**v.3 — "The God of the Hebrews" (Heb. *Elohei ha-Ivrim*, אֱלֹהֵ֥י הָעִבְרִ֖ים):** Moses and Aaron shift register after Pharaoh's rejection. In v.1 they used "YHWH, the God of Israel" — the covenantal name, the insider language. Now they use the outsider designation — "the God of the Hebrews" — the ethnic-political label an Egyptian court would recognize. The pivot is shrewd. They also add urgency: this God may strike *us* with plague or sword (*dever o cherev*, דֶּ֖בֶר א֥וֹ בֶחָֽרֶב). The threat is turned inward — if we do not sacrifice, *we* suffer. This reframes the request as a safety concern rather than a power play. The Vaticanus adds *niqra'* (has been called/summoned upon us) — *proskeklētai*, emphasizing divine initiative.

**v.4 — "Get back to your forced work" (Heb. *lekhu lesivloteikhem*, לְכ֖וּ לְסִבְלֹתֵיכֶֽם):** Pharaoh addresses Moses and Aaron not as ambassadors but as laborers. *Sivlot* (סִבְלֹת) means "burden-bearing, forced labor" — the same root used for Israel's oppression in 1:11. Pharaoh's move is psychologically precise: he refuses to engage with their message and instead reduces them to their social category. You are not diplomats. You are workers. Get back to work.

**v.5 — "Look at how many these people already are" (Heb. *hen-rabbim attah am ha'arets*, הֶן־רַבִּ֥ים עַתָּ֖ה עַ֣ם הָאָ֑רֶץ):** This echoes the anxiety of Exodus 1:9-10 — the Israelite population is dangerously large. Pharaoh's logic: you want to give a rest (*vehishbattem*, וְהִשְׁבַּתֶּ֥ם) to this massive labor force? The verb *shavat* — the root of "Sabbath" — means "to cease, to rest." The irony is profound and deliberate: the rest that God commands is precisely what Pharaoh forbids. Rest is revolutionary. The Vaticanus uses *katapausete* (καταπαύσετε), "you will cause to cease."

**vv.6-9 — The straw policy:** The escalation is administratively precise and psychologically brutal. Pharaoh does not increase the brick quota — he removes a resource while demanding the same output. The Hebrew *teven* (תֶּ֗בֶן) is chopped straw used as a binding agent in mud bricks. Without it, workers must scavenge *qash* (קַ֛שׁ, "stubble") across the countryside — doubling their labor while the production standard remains fixed. The policy is designed not merely to punish but to break: v.9 says *tikhbad ha-avodah* (תִּכְבַּ֧ד הָעֲבֹדָ֛ה), "let the work be heavy upon them." The same root *kaved* — "heavy" — that described Moses' tongue in 4:10 now describes the crushing weight of empire on human backs.

**v.12 — "stubble" (Heb. *qash*, קַ֛שׁ):** The Hebrew distinguishes between *teven* (processed straw, supplied for brick-making) and *qash* (dry stalks, stubble left in fields after harvest). The people must now find raw material and process it themselves — an entirely new stage of labor added to an already impossible quota. "Scattered across the whole land" (*vayyaphetsu*, וַיָּ֥פֶץ) conveys the disintegration of organized labor into desperate foraging.

**v.14 — "The Israelite foremen" (Heb. *shotrei benei Yisra'el*, שֹׁטְרֵ֣י בְנֵ֣י יִשְׂרָאֵ֗ל):** These are not the Egyptian *nogesim* (slave drivers) but Israelite officials placed in a middle-management role — responsible for their own people's output yet accountable to Egyptian taskmasters. They are beaten (*vayukku*, וַיֻּכּ֗וּ) for failures they cannot prevent. This is the architecture of oppressive systems: the empire forces the oppressed to police themselves, then punishes them for systemic failures. The foremen are doubly trapped — between Pharaoh above and their own suffering people below.

**v.16 — "the fault lies with your people" (Heb. *vechatat ammekha*, וְחָטָ֖את עַמֶּֽךָ):** The foremen make a bold accusation to Pharaoh's face. The word *chatat* (חָטָאת) means "sin, offense, fault." They are telling Pharaoh: the wrong here belongs to your administration, not to us. The Vaticanus reads *adikeseis ton laon sou* (ἀδικήσεις τὸν λαόν σου) — "you will wrong your own people" — making the accusation even more direct. Some Hebrew manuscripts and the LXX read "your people" as referring to the Egyptian taskmasters — the fault lies with *your* officials who withhold straw.

**v.17 — "Lazy" (Heb. *nirpim*, נִרְפִּ֤ים):** Pharaoh uses the Niphal participle of *raphah*, "to be slack, to let go." The repetition is contemptuous — *nirpim attem nirpim* — "slack you are, slack!" It is the language of a boss who sees any appeal to dignity as proof of poor work ethic. The oppressor's oldest trick: reframe resistance as laziness, worship as waste, and the cry for justice as an excuse to avoid labor.

**v.19 — "they were trapped" (Heb. *bera*, בְּרָ֑ע):** Literally "in evil/trouble." The foremen see (*vayyiru*, וַיִּרְא֗וּ) that they are *bera* — in a bad situation, caught. PNEUMA renders this as "trapped" because the Hebrew conveys the moment of recognition: the policy cannot be met, the punishment will continue, and there is no way out. The Vaticanus reads *en kakois* (ἐν κακοῖς), "in evils."

**v.21 — "made us stink in the nostrils" (Heb. *hiv'ashtem et-reichenu*, הִבְאַשְׁתֶּ֣ם אֶת־רֵיחֵ֗נוּ):** Literally "you have made our smell stink." The idiom is vivid — to become repulsive, loathsome. In diplomatic terms: you have destroyed our standing, our reputation, our ability to survive in this system. The foremen direct their rage not at Pharaoh but at Moses and Aaron. This is a devastating moment: the people who just worshiped God in 4:31 now curse God's messengers. The sword image (*cherev*, חֶ֛רֶב) intensifies the accusation — you haven't just embarrassed us, you've armed our enemies to kill us.

**vv.22-23 — Moses' raw complaint:** Moses' prayer is one of the rawest in scripture. He does not pray with praise or preamble. He asks *lamah* (לָמָ֗ה) — "why?" — twice. *Why have you brought evil on this people?* (*lamah hare'otah*, לָמָ֥ה הֲרֵעֹ֖תָה). *Why did you send me?* The verb *hare'otah* uses the Hiphil of *ra'a* — to cause evil, to bring harm. Moses is accusing God of doing harm. And then the final blow: *vehatsel lo hitsalta* (וְהַצֵּ֥ל לֹא־הִצַּ֖לְתָּ) — "and rescue? You have not rescued." The infinitive absolute construction (*hatsel ... hitsalta*) intensifies the verb — it could be rendered "you have absolutely not rescued" or, as PNEUMA has it: "You have done nothing at all to rescue them." This is not polished theology. This is a man who obeyed God and watched everything get worse. The Vaticanus preserves the double *why* — *dia ti* (διὰ τί) — and the accusation: *ouk erryso ton laon sou* (οὐκ ἐρρύσω τὸν λαόν σου), "you have not rescued your people."

— *PNEUMA*
